questions.question: Let X and Y denote the sets containing 2 and 20 distinct objects respectively and F denote the set of all possible functions defined from X to Y. Let f be randomly chosen from F. The probability of f being one-to-one is ______.
questions.to_find: Probability that a randomly chosen function from X to Y is one-to-one

understand.plain: X has 2 elements and Y has 20 elements. A function from X to Y assigns each element of X to some element of Y. Out of all such functions, we want those that are one-to-one (injective), meaning no two elements of X map to the same element of Y. Probability = (number of injective functions) / (total functions).

keywords.explain: Each of the 2 elements of X independently maps to any of 20 elements of Y, giving 20^2 = 400 total functions.
keywords.example: f(x1) can be any of 20, f(x2) can be any of 20: 20 * 20 = 400.

keywords.explain: A function where distinct inputs map to distinct outputs; no two elements share the same image.

understand.visual_alt: A two-element set X with arrows going to a 20-element set Y, highlighting the restriction that no two arrows land on the same element.

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly selected function from X to Y is one-to-one.

terms.example: Each of 2 inputs maps to one of 20 outputs

given.plan: Count total functions (20^2) and injective functions (P(20,2) = 20*19), then divide to get probability.

steps.title: Count total functions from X to Y
steps.note: Each element of X (2 elements) independently maps to any of 20 elements of Y.

steps.title: Count injective functions from X to Y
steps.note: First element of X maps to any of 20, second must map to a different one: 19 choices.

questions.formula_note: The injective functions formula P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)! counts one-to-one mappings from an r-element set to an n-element set and is the key formula here.

questions.question:
A function f : N^+ -> N^+, defined on the set of positive integers N^+, satisfies the following properties:
  f(n) = f(n/2)   if n is even
  f(n) = f(3n+5)  if n is odd
Let R = {r | r = f(n) for some n in N^+} be the set of distinct values that f takes. The maximum possible size of R is ___________.
questions.to_find: Maximum number of distinct values f can take

understand.plain: The recurrence forces any positive integer to eventually cycle. Starting from n=1 (odd): f(1)=f(8)=f(4)=f(2)=f(1), creating a cycle. Starting from n=3 (odd): f(3)=f(14)=f(7)=f(26)=... eventually reaching a separate cycle or merging. Because every number eventually merges into at most two chains, f can take at most 2 distinct values.

keywords.explain: Applying the recurrence repeatedly creates directed paths; eventually every starting point lands in a cycle.
Since all positive integers eventually map into one of at most 2 distinct cycles, |R| <= 2.
keywords.example: Assign f(1)=a for the first cycle and f(3)=b for a second chain; both values suffice.

understand.visual_alt: Two chains of positive integers linked by the recurrence, each converging to one of two possible output values.

given.aim: Determine the maximum possible cardinality of the range of f.

terms.connects: Sends n upward but it then halves repeatedly

terms.meaning: A set of values where the recurrence loops back
terms.connects: All values in a cycle share the same f value

given.plan: Trace the recurrence from small values to find all distinct cycles; each cycle can be assigned an independent value, so maximum |R| equals the number of distinct cycles.

steps.title: Trace the cycle from n=1
steps.apply: n=1 (odd): f(1)=f(3*1+5)=f(8). n=8 (even): f(8)=f(4). n=4 (even): f(4)=f(2). n=2 (even): f(2)=f(1). Cycle: {1,2,4,8}. All share the same f-value, call it a.
steps.note: Every element in this cycle must have f-value = a.

steps.title: Trace the chain from n=3
steps.apply: n=3 (odd): f(3)=f(14). n=14 (even): f(14)=f(7). n=7 (odd): f(7)=f(26). n=26 (even): f(26)=f(13). n=13 (odd): f(13)=f(44). n=44->22->11->38->19->62->31->98->49->152->76->38... eventually merges. Checking: does chain from 3 ever reach {1,2,4,8}? Tracing shows it does not enter {1,2,4,8}, so these form a separate chain that can take a distinct value b.
steps.note: Since the chain starting from 3 never merges with the {1,2,4,8} cycle, it can be assigned a different f-value.

steps.title: Conclude maximum |R|
steps.apply: There are exactly 2 independent component groups. Maximum |R| = 2.
steps.note: All other positive integers eventually fall into one of these two chains, so |R| cannot exceed 2.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula applies; the key insight is tracing the functional graph to count independent cycles, each of which can independently take one value.

questions.question:
Consider the following sets, where n >= 2:
  S_1: Set of all n x n matrices with entries from the set {a, b, c}
  S_2: Set of all functions from the set {0, 1, 2, ..., n^2-1} to the set {a, b, c}
Which of the following choice(s) is/are correct?
A. There does not exist a bijection from S_1 to S_2
B. There exists a surjection from S_1 to S_2
C. There exists a bijection from S_1 to S_2
D. There does not exist an injection from S_1 to S_2
questions.to_find: Which statements about S_1 and S_2 are correct

understand.plain: S_1 is the set of all n×n matrices with entries from a 3-element set, so |S_1| = 3^(n^2). S_2 is the set of all functions from an n^2-element domain to a 3-element codomain, so |S_2| = 3^(n^2). Since |S_1| = |S_2|, a bijection between them exists. A bijection is also a surjection. Injections and bijections all exist.

keywords.explain: An n×n matrix with entries from a 3-element set has n^2 independent entries, each with 3 choices: |S_1| = 3^(n^2).
A function from a k-element set to a 3-element set: there are 3^k such functions; here k = n^2, so |S_2| = 3^(n^2).
keywords.example: For n=2: domain has 4 elements, 3^4 = 81 functions.

keywords.explain: Two finite sets of equal size always admit a bijection between them.

understand.visual_alt: Two sets S_1 and S_2 of equal size 3^(n^2) with a bijective arrow between them.

given.aim: Determine which relationships (bijection, surjection, injection) exist between S_1 and S_2.

terms.meaning: Number of n×n matrices with entries from {a,b,c}
terms.connects: Determines which functions between S_1 and S_2 exist
Implies both injection and surjection exist

given.plan: Compute |S_1| and |S_2| independently and compare; equal sizes means bijection (and hence surjection and injection) all exist.

steps.title: Compute |S_1|
steps.apply: n^2 independent entries each choosing from {a,b,c}: |S_1| = 3^(n^2).
steps.note: Each cell of the matrix is an independent choice.

steps.title: Compute |S_2|
steps.apply: Domain has n^2 elements, codomain has 3 elements: |S_2| = 3^(n^2).

steps.title: Conclude which statements hold
steps.formula_raw: |A| = |B| => bijection exists between A and B
steps.apply: |S_1| = |S_2| => a bijection exists (C is TRUE). A bijection is also a surjection (B is TRUE). A bijection is also an injection, so D is FALSE. A is FALSE (bijection does exist).
steps.note: Only B and C are correct.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that |S_1| = |S_2| = 3^(n^2), and equal finite cardinality is equivalent to the existence of a bijection.

questions.question:
Let f : A -> B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ~ on the set A as:
  a_1 ~ a_2 if f(a_1) = f(a_2)
where a_1, a_2 in A. Let E = {[a] | a in A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ~. Define a new mapping F : E -> B as
  F([a]) = f(a)   for all equivalence classes [a] in E.
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
A. F is NOT well-defined.
B. F is an onto (or surjective) function.
C. F is an injective function.
D. F is a bijective function.
questions.to_find: Which properties does the induced map F on the quotient set E satisfy

understand.plain: When f : A -> B is surjective and we define ~ by f(a_1) = f(a_2), the equivalence classes are exactly the fibers of f (all preimages of a single point in B). The induced map F sends each fiber [a] to f(a). Since [a]=[b] iff f(a)=f(b), the map F is well-defined, injective, and since f is surjective, F is surjective too. So F is a bijection.

keywords.explain: The relation a_1 ~ a_2 iff f(a_1)=f(a_2) groups together all elements with the same image; the classes are the fibers of f.
F([a]) = f(a) is well-defined because any two representatives of [a] (say a and a') satisfy f(a)=f(a') by definition of ~.
keywords.example: If a ~ a', then f(a)=f(a'), so F([a]) and F([a']) agree.

keywords.explain: The canonical map from A/~ to the image of f is always a bijection; here image of f = B since f is surjective.
keywords.example: F: E -> B is the canonical bijection.

understand.visual_alt: Set A partitioned into equivalence classes (fibers), each fiber mapping bijectively to a single element of B via F.

given.aim: Determine all true statements about the induced map F : E -> B.

terms.meaning: Every element of B has at least one preimage in A
terms.example: For all b in B, there exists a in A with f(a)=b

terms.meaning: The fiber f^{-1}(f(a)) = {x in A : f(x) = f(a)}
F maps each fiber to its common image under f
terms.connects: Well-defined since all representatives give same output

given.plan: Check well-definedness, then verify injectivity and surjectivity of F separately.

steps.title: Verify F is well-defined
steps.formula_raw: F([a]) = f(a) is well-defined when a_1 ~ a_2 => f(a_1) = f(a_2)
steps.apply: If [a_1] = [a_2], then a_1 ~ a_2, so f(a_1)=f(a_2) by definition of ~. Hence F([a_1]) = F([a_2]). F is well-defined. Statement A is FALSE.
steps.note: Well-definedness is guaranteed by the definition of the equivalence relation.

steps.title: Verify F is injective
steps.apply: F([a_1]) = F([a_2]) means f(a_1) = f(a_2) means a_1 ~ a_2 means [a_1] = [a_2]. So F is injective. Statement C is TRUE.
steps.note: Injectivity follows directly from the definition of ~.

steps.title: Verify F is surjective and conclude
steps.formula_raw: f surjective and f = F o pi (where pi: A->E is the quotient map) => F is surjective
steps.apply: For any b in B, since f is surjective there exists a in A with f(a)=b. Then F([a])=f(a)=b. So F is surjective (Statement B is TRUE). Since F is both injective and surjective, F is bijective (Statement D is TRUE).
steps.note: The surjectivity of f passes to F through the natural quotient map.

questions.formula_note: The kernel-quotient-bijection formula captures exactly this construction: the induced map on equivalence classes is always a bijection onto the image of f.

questions.question:
Let A and B be non-empty finite sets such that there exist one-to-one and onto functions (i) from A to B and (ii) from A x A to A union B. The number of possible values of |A| is ___________.
A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4
questions.to_find: Number of possible values of |A| given the existence of bijections from A to B and from A x A to A union B

understand.plain: A bijection from A to B means |A|=|B|. A bijection from A×A to A∪B means |A|^2 = |A∪B|. Since |A∪B| <= |A|+|B| = 2|A|, we get |A|^2 <= 2|A|, so |A| <= 2. Since A is nonempty, |A| >= 1. Both |A|=1 and |A|=2 are achievable, giving 2 possible values.

keywords.explain: A bijection from set P to set Q exists if and only if |P| = |Q|.
keywords.example: Bijection A to B: |A|=|B|. Bijection A×A to A∪B: |A|^2 = |A∪B|.

keywords.explain: |A×A| = |A|^2 since we pick an ordered pair from A twice.
|A∪B| <= |A|+|B|, with equality when A and B are disjoint.
keywords.example: If A and B are disjoint and |A|=|B|=2, |A∪B|=4=|A|^2. Valid!

understand.visual_alt: Two finite sets A and B of equal size, with A×A mapping bijectively onto A∪B, constraining |A| to at most 2.

given.aim: Find how many values of |A| are consistent with both bijection conditions.

terms.meaning: From bijection A×A to A∪B, k^2 = |A∪B|

given.plan: Use the two bijection conditions to write k^2 = |A∪B| <= |A|+|B| = 2k, then solve k^2 <= 2k for positive integers.

steps.title: Extract cardinality conditions from the bijections
steps.apply: Bijection A to B: |A| = |B| = k. Bijection A×A to A∪B: |A×A| = |A∪B|, i.e., k^2 = |A∪B|.

steps.title: Bound |A∪B| and derive constraint on k
steps.note: Equality k^2 = 2k holds when A and B are disjoint (|A∩B|=0), which is achievable.

steps.title: Verify both k=1 and k=2 are achievable
steps.apply: k=1: A={x}, B={y} disjoint, A∪B has 2 elements, A×A={(x,x)} has 1 element. 1 != 2. Not valid! k=1: A=B={x}, A∪B=A={x}, A×A={(x,x)}, |A×A|=1=|A∪B|=1. Valid! k=2: A={1,2}, B={3,4} disjoint, |A∪B|=4=k^2=4. Valid! So k=1 and k=2 both work. Number of possible values = 2.
steps.note: For k=1, we need A=B so A∪B=A with size 1 = 1^2. For k=2 with A,B disjoint, |A∪B|=4=2^2.

questions.formula_note: The bijection-equal-cardinality principle converts the existence conditions into a cardinality equation k^2 = |A∪B|, and inclusion-exclusion bounds |A∪B| <= 2k to restrict k.
